response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

persona.time_onsocialmedia: You spend a few times monthly on social media

response_parsed.opinion: As a woman in my late thirties who works retail in Milan, I've noticed that fake content on social media can be really draining, especially when it's tied to the kind of emotions I'm dealing with right now. I'm not super active online, only a few times a month, but when I do, it's hard to avoid the kind of posts that make me feel like I'm not enough. My parents didn't go to college, they had vocational training and lower secondary education, so I don't have a lot of experience with the digital world. But I do know how to spot some of the red flags—like when a post has a lot of emojis or when it's too perfect. I've tried to limit my time on social media because it makes me feel anxious, but sometimes I see something that makes me want to share it with friends. I'm a bit depressed, so I need to be careful not to let fake content affect my mood too much. I think the best thing is to just delete the post and move on, but it's tough because sometimes the posts are so convincing. I've also found that talking to my husband helps—sometimes he can see things I miss. I try to focus on the things that make me happy, like watching a good movie or playing a game, instead of getting stuck on what's online. It's important to remember that not everything online is real, and that's okay. I don't want to spend too much time worrying about it, but I do want to protect my mental health.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona is a 41-year-old woman in Milan who works retail, has a lower secondary education, and is currently depressed. Her low openness score (38) means she's not very open to new ideas, but her high agreeableness (97) and conscientiousness (75) suggest she's kind and organized. Her high neuroticism (89) means she's prone to anxiety and stress, which makes fake content more distressing. She spends only a few times a month on social media, so she's not heavily affected, but she's vulnerable to emotional manipulation. The opinion focuses on practical steps she'd take: deleting posts, limiting time, talking to her husband, and focusing on hobbies. It avoids technical jargon, uses relatable examples (like emojis), and reflects her depression and anxiety without being overly clinical.
citations: Mental health resources for retail workers
